perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall, upon termination of this Agreement, or upon written request of Discloser, whichever is earlier, immediately, but not later than 10 days after any notice thereof by Discloser, return (or destroy at Discloser’s option) all copies of such Discloser’s Confidential Information and certify in writing its compliance with this requirement, except that Recipient may retain a copy of such Confidential Information solely for archival purpose .
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall, upon termination of this Agreement, or upon written request of Discloser, whichever is earlier, immediately, but not later than 60 days after any notice thereof by Discloser, return (or destroy at Discloser’s option) all copies of such Discloser’s Confidential Information and certify in writing its compliance with this requirement, except that Recipient may retain a copy of such Confidential Information solely for archival purpose .
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the return of confidential information within 10 days of termination or request. Changing this to 60 days contradicts the general principle that contracts should be performed within a reasonable timeframe, and what constitutes 'reasonable' can be subject to legal interpretation based on industry standards and the nature of the information. This may cause issues in cases where there is trade secrets involved, which are often time-sensitive.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA aims to protect trade secrets, which are often time-sensitive. Delaying the return of confidential information to 60 days after notice could allow for unauthorized use or disclosure of trade secrets, conflicting with the intent to protect such information from misappropriation within a reasonable timeframe.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1839 - Definitions U.S. Code Notes prev | next As used in this chapter— (1) the term “ foreign instrumentality ” means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term “ foreign agent ” means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of a foreign government; (3) the term “ trade secret ” means all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if— (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information; (4) the term “ owner ”, with respect to a trade secret , means the person or entity in whom or in which rightful legal or equitable title to, or license in, the trade secret is reposed; (5) the term “ misappropriation ” means— (A) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means ; or (B) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who— (i) used improper means to a

perturbation.justification: The change from 10 days to 60 days for returning confidential information creates an inconsistency regarding the reasonable timeframe for protecting trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the UTSA's aim to prevent misappropriation. Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By extending the return period, the modified contract increases the risk of unauthorized use or disclosure, thus undermining the protection intended by trade secret laws. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both indicate how delaying the return of confidential information could lead to unauthorized use or disclosure of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be effective from the Effective Date until 24 months thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be effective from the Effective Date until 6 months thereafter.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the agreement's term from 24 months to 6 months can be problematic, especially if the confidential information shared has a longer useful life or requires protection beyond that timeframe. A shorter term may not adequately protect trade secrets or other sensitive information, creating a legal risk.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (DTSA)
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA provides federal protection for trade secrets. If the confidential information disclosed under this NDA constitutes a trade secret with a commercially viable lifespan beyond 6 months, limiting the NDA's term to 6 months could be deemed inadequate to protect those trade secrets, potentially undermining the protections intended by the DTSA, especially related to misappropriation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the agreement term from 24 months to 6 months. The Law Explanation says this undermines protections intended by the DTSA, especially related to misappropriation, because confidential information's commercially viable lifespan could exceed 6 months. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the Economic Espionage Act of 1996, suggesting the importance of protecting trade secrets, which the shorter term could fail to do. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a time-sensitive obligation (agreement term) does not align with legal requirements (trade secret protection timeframe), making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement for any or no reason upon 10 days written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement for any or no reason upon 1 day written notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for termination from 10 days to 1 day is problematic. This change significantly shortens the time available for the receiving party to take necessary steps to protect the discloser's confidential information upon notice of termination. This could lead to potential breaches of confidentiality and disputes over the use of information during that short window.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The covenant of good faith and fair dealing is implied in every contract. Reducing the termination notice to one day may be interpreted as a breach of this covenant, especially if the short notice period causes undue hardship or prevents the receiving party from taking reasonable steps to protect the disclosing party's confidential information, effectively sabotaging the original intent of the agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period for termination from 10 days to 1 day. The Law Explanation states this may violate the covenant of good faith and fair dealing, especially if it causes undue hardship or prevents the receiving party from protecting confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing "honest dealing" and "observance of fair dealing standards." Reducing the notice period so drastically could be interpreted as a failure to act in good faith, contradicting the implied covenant within the contract.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient’s duty to protect Discloser’s Confidential Information expires 5 years from the date on which that Confidential Information was disclosed to Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient’s duty to protect Discloser’s Confidential Information expires 1 year from the date on which that Confidential Information was disclosed to Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the protection period to 1 year might not be sufficient for certain types of confidential information, especially trade secrets, which can have a lifespan exceeding this period. Such a short protection period could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable, particularly if it undermines the value and proprietary nature of the disclosed information.
perturbation.law_explanation: If the confidential information includes trade secrets with a reasonable lifespan beyond one year, limiting the protection period to one year could be seen as insufficient to protect the trade secrets from misappropriation. The UTSA aims to protect trade secrets for as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage, and a one-year period could be deemed unreasonably short.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the protection period for confidential information from 5 years to 1 year. The Law Explanation states that if confidential information includes trade secrets with a reasonable lifespan beyond one year, limiting the protection period to one year could be seen as insufficient to protect the trade secrets from misappropriation. Although the Scraped Law Snippets do not directly address the duration of trade secret protection, the Law Explanation draws on the UTSA's aim to protect trade secrets for as long as they maintain a competitive advantage. The reduction to one year directly undermines this goal, creating a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation further reinforces this by stating that the short protection period could be deemed unreasonable and unenforceable, particularly if it undermines the value and proprietary nature of the disclosed information, thus creating a contradiction.
